issue_title: STLLoader loads only the first mesh
issue_body:
Hi,

I have a feature request (or maybe a bug report depending on your point of view) for the .stl file importer. Let me start with a brief background. I use CADMesh (github.com/christopherpoole/cadmesh) to load meshed CAD models into a Monte Carlo simulation toolkit called Geant4 (geant4.cern.ch). Each model is essentially a collection of volume elements, aka "cells". In Geant4, I assign a material to the model and simulate how much energy is deposited in each cell when some particles pass through.

I save models in .stl format. A simple example is attached. It is a 100x100x30 mm^3 slab divided into 2x2x2 cells. As you can see, the file contains eight meshes named cell1 ... cell8. Each mesh begins with e.g. "solid cell1" and ends with e.g. "endsolid cell1". CADMesh uses Assimp to load the file.

Expected behavior: Loading the .stl file with Importer::ReadFile should yield a scene with eight meshes named cell1 ... cell8. Each mesh should correspond to one volume element.

Observed behavior: Loading the .stl file yields only one mesh. This mesh is whichever one happened to be the first in the file (cell1 in this case) because STLLoader stops reading upon encountering the first instance of "endsolid". The mesh has no name; The name is instead assigned to the root node.

I note that all the models in test/models/STL/ have exactly one mesh, so perhaps this behavior has gone under the radar so far. In my case, it is not uncommon for a model to have 100,000 meshes. I should point out that the behavior described above is for ASCII files. For binary files, it seems that all cells are loaded, but they are merged into a single mesh. I'm not exactly sure about what goes on under the hood on this one, though, because the resulting cell doesn't have the volume (physically, in mm^3) that it should. I'm probably missing some subtlety.

I hope I have correctly understood the expected behavior of Assimp and I'm happy to answer any questions if my request is unclear. Thanks in advance!

//
Anders
It would be fine to support FBX format > 7300 :)

issue_title: Wrong line numbers reported in find-untranslated
issue_body:
See remarks by @vincentfretin in pull request #32.
It is basically this problem in lxml and maybe most xml libraries: https://bugs.launchpad.net/lxml/+bug/526799

Take this file, simplified from the `confirm.pt` that Vincent points to:

```
<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" xml:lang="en"
      xmlns:i18n="http://xml.zope.org/namespaces/i18n"
      lang="en"
      i18n:domain="plone">
<body>
  <input
      type="submit"
      value="Confirm action"
      name="form.button.confirm"
      class="standalone" />
</body>
</html>
```

Running find-untranslated on it complains about line 3:

```
confirm.pt:3:2:
-ERROR- - value attribute of <... submit/button> lacks i18n:attribute
```

This is because lxml turns the content into:

```
<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" xmlns:i18n="http://xml.zope.org/namespaces/i18n" xml:lang="en" lang="en" i18n:domain="plone">
<body>
  <input type="submit" value="Confirm action" name="form.button.confirm" class="standalone"/>
</body>
</html>
```

So every tag tag is now on one line instead of possibly multiple.

issue_title: Wrapping does not work when string contains newline
issue_body:
Hello,

I made the observation, that wrapping does not work correctly, when the message string contains an explicit newline character ('\n').
Interestingly I was not able to replicated this false behavior by writing a new test (https://github.com/staeff/i18ndude/commit/8358f1240a3ac3db0d2b0230b8f8f903c95b9a4e). So the problem seems not to occur in `wrap_string` in `utils.py` (https://github.com/collective/i18ndude/blob/master/src/i18ndude/utils.py#L99) but somewhere else along the way.

To replicate the false behavior you could run the following steps. You should see the problem by having a look at the output of the git diff.

```bash
$ git clone https://gist.github.com/b9adc20c725521516318.git i18ndudetest
$ cd i18ndudetest
$ i18ndude sync --wrap --pot test.pot test.po  # i18ndude needs to be in $PATH
$ git diff
 msgid "ok"
 msgstr ""
-"Falls aktiv wird der entsprechende Linkcycle einen Button haben, mit dem man d
+"Falls aktiv wird der entsprechende Linkcycle einen Button haben, mit dem man "
+"durch ihn blättern kann. mit dem man durch ihn blättern kann. mit dem man "
+"durch ihn blättern kann. mit dem man durch ihn blättern kann."

 msgid "problematic"
 msgstr ""
-"Falls aktiv wird der entsprechende \nLinkcycle einen Button haben, mit dem man
+"Falls aktiv wird der entsprechende \n"
+"Linkcycle einen Button haben, mit dem man durch ihn blättern kann. mit dem man
```
I would be willing to investigate this problem further, hints where to look for the error are appreciated.
Ping @eventbritemitch

issue_title: Difflib and python-Levenshtein give different ratios in some cases
issue_body:
This is probably known and accepted, but since it's not mentioned in the docs I'll raise this anyway. In certain edge cases, difflib and python-Levenshtein give different ratios.
E.g.:
```
>>> from fuzzywuzzy import fuzz, StringMatcher
>>> import difflib

#As long as python-Levenshtein is available, that will be used for the following:
>>> fuzz.ratio("ababab", "aaaaab")
67

#Switch to difflib:
>>> fuzz.SequenceMatcher = difflib.SequenceMatcher
>>> fuzz.ratio("ababab", "aaaaab")
33
```

I don't know how python-Levenshtein works, but difflib first chooses the left-most longest block in the first sequence that matches any block in the second sequence. Then among the longest matches in the second sequence to this chosen block, it will choose the left-most.

What this means is that it is forced to choose the first "ab" in the first string, and then matches it to the only "ab" in the second, at the end. This means it cannot recurse either to the left or the right to look for more matching blocks, since this match is at the very beginning of the first string and at the very end of the second. So the ratio calculated is 33, since it has matched one third (4/12) of the total characters.

I'm assuming that in python-Levenshtein, it makes a decision about which matches to choose based not just on maximality and left-most-ness, but also on whether the pair of matches chosen allows subsequent recursion. This is because it scores 67, so must have matched eight of 12 characters, i.e. it must have matched "ab" and then two subsequent "a"s in each sequence, meaning it must have chosen the last "ab" in the first string. Either this or it can recurse to opposite sides in the two sequences but that surely isn't happening.

To show this, if we change the second sequence to "abaaaa", difflib will also score 67 (since it matches the first two characters of each sequence then recurses to the right). See as follows:

```
>>> fuzz.ratio("ababab", "abaaaa")
67

#And switching pack to python-Levenshtein, no change:
>>> fuzz.SequenceMatcher = fuzzywuzzy.StringMatcher.StringMatcher
>>> fuzz.ratio("ababab", "abaaaa")
67
```

issue_title: preparer cannot call under subclassed SchemaNode
issue_body:
`colander.interfaces.Preparer` says preparer has one argument.
However, when a SchemaNode is subclass-ed, `preparer.__call__(value)` requires two arguments (`self` and `value`) and raises exception.


```python
import colander as c

def global_preparer(value):
    return 'global_prepared:' + value


class Schema1(c.Schema):
    node = c.SchemaNode(typ=c.Str(), preparer=global_preparer)


schema1 = Schema1().bind()
deserialized = schema1.deserialize({'node': 'test'})
print('Schema1:', deserialized)
# printed
# Schema1: {'node': 'global_prepared:test'}

class Node(c.SchemaNode):
    schema_type = c.Str
    preparer = global_preparer


class Schema2(c.Schema):
    node = Node()


schema2 = Schema2().bind()
deserialized = schema2.deserialize({'node': 'test'})
# exceptioned
# TypeError: global_preparer() takes 1 positional argument but 2 were given
```

Speaking of adoption (previous thread), I think a very impactful feature would be to have a SQAlchemy connector to `ctds`.

I have been on a quest for the perfect Python MSSQL driver for the last few months and each driver has  issues:
* `pymssql`: No bulk insert support + stability issues reported for 1+ years (which crashes/seg-faults the python process). Pros: multi platform support in conda.
* `pyodbc`: No bulk insert support + not easy to setup. Some support in conda.
* `pytds`: Does have bulk insert support, but does not support SSL connections, which makes it unusable on Azure. Pros: pure python, easy to deploy.
* `ctds`: No SQLAlchemy connector. Not sure if it supports SSL either? Also, is it cross-plarform? It looks like there are some conda packages available for OS X and Linux but did not find anything for Windows. 

I feel that the convenience of having a pre-compiled conda package is huge, especially when people using Python+SQL Server are frequently data scientist kind of personas, and tend to favor convenience above everything else.

A SQLAlchemy connector allows more advance users to easily swap their driver. I tried to write one for `ctds` but pretty much failed.

Finally, I don't think a lot of people know about `ctds`. Having a blog article on Zillow's tech blog (if it exists!) would definitely give it much more visibility.

issue_title: Python library not compatible with HMAC, Base64 in Python 3.4
issue_body:
When attempting to use the library in a Python 3.4 environment, the _sign_request() function will always return ERR_UNKNOWN. This comes down to the _sign_vals() function passing str objects to the underlying functions, instead of byte objects.

Underlying errors include:
TypeError: 'str' does not support the buffer interface
TypeError: Unicode-objects must be encoded before hashing

Re-writing _sign_vals() to look like this:

    def _sign_vals(key, vals, prefix, expire):
      exp = str(int(time.time()) + expire)

      val = '|'.join(vals + [ exp ])
      b64 = base64.b64encode(str.encode(val))
      cookie = '%s|%s' % (prefix, bytes.decode(b64))

      sig = _hmac_sha1(str.encode(key), str.encode(cookie))
      return '%s|%s' % (cookie, sig)

works just fine in Python 3.4, and in manual testing of the same functions in 2.7.

```python
    def validate_option(self, key):

            raise ConfigException(msg)

issue_title: Allow `Get Element Attribute` to support using a webelement as a locator
issue_body:
As [suggested by](https://github.com/rtomac/robotframework-selenium2library/issues/315#issuecomment-58188151) @ombre42,
 
> Still analyzing, but wanted to suggest changing Get Element Attribute to support using a webelement as a locator. It would be easy to do in a backwards compatible way by adding a name argument that supersedes parsing attribute_locator for an attribute name.

As our team has recently expanded, it would be good to have a goto list of guidelines for collaborators. 

Here's a few things I think it should have

- Never commit directly to master
- Never merge a PR that fails CI
- Never merge a PR that adds functionality but doesn't properly test it
- Ensure the changelog is updated if any notable change is made
- Try to get others to review changes before merging

This is just a start and I would love to hear suggestions and improvements.

@pekkaklarck It would also be beneficial to know how RF handles this.

issue_title: Improper URL encoding in geocoding queries
Add image export function to viz classes
issue_body:
A common data science workflow is to include a static image version of a visual for sharing or screen shot creating.  

This could be added as a `saveImage()` HTML button on a `viz` class object that downloads the current map viewport as a PNG, or it could be a viz.toImage() python function that outputs a PNG of the viz at the stated zoom, center, pitch, and bearing.

issue_title: Default JSONEncoder won't serialize iterators, can't easily be extended.
issue_body:
http://code.google.com/p/simplejson/issues/detail?id=88

Reported by i...@simplegeo.com, Jan 17, 2011
What steps will reproduce the problem?
1. simplejson.dumps(iter(range(10)))

What is the expected output? What do you see instead?
The iterator should generate a JSON serialized list.

This is a somewhat thorny issue. I have a large dataset which is lazily loaded in via a generator. I'd like to avoid materializing the entire dataset before serializing, ideally by having SimpleJSON consume from my input generator and write to a FD via dump().

The extension mechanism via `default=some_method' doesn't work, because I need to yield individual elements from the seq, not a materialized seq. The existing _iterencode_list() method is totally capable of serializing lazy seqs, but _iterencode() lacks the necessary conditional to invoke it for those types — and the number of possible types is extremely large. Every method in itertools, for example, returns a different type.

I was able to make this work with stdlib's json module, though it was not pretty. I had to subclass JSONEncoder, copy-paste _iterencode() in, and add the necessary conditional to send lazy seqs through _iterencode_list(). This approach is significantly more painful with SimpleJSON, since _iterencode() and friends are now hidden inside _make_iterencode(), where they can't be touched — I'd have to copy-paste the entire method (and its inner methods) to add one more conditional.

I understand that there's going to be some level of heuristic to detect these types, due to the lack of a shared base type for lazy seqs, but is there not some better way to handle this?

CFFI extensions compiled for PyPy do not export ``'init' + modname`` nor ``'PyInit_' + modname`` symbols but instead they export ``'_cffi_pypyinit_' + modname`` and thus they are skipped by ``auditwheel``. However actually ``auditwheel`` can repair them. As an example one may take a look at ``python-snappy`` - it uses cffi for PyPy. And proof for using ``_cffi_pypyinit_`` prefix may be found at ``cffi/recompiler.py``.

issue_title: setup.py install doesn't work for packages with setup_requires on older macOS
issue_body:
SInce the TLS 1.0/1.1 turndown, `easy_install` doesn't work on older macOS, because the builtin libraries don't support SSL.  For the most part, we just use latest pip instead, and things work fine.  But when installing a package with `setup_requires` from source (e.g. `pip install git+git://github.com/pycqa/flake8.git@master`), we run into trouble even on latest pip/setuptools, because pip runs `setup.py install`, which in turn runs `easy_install`, which fails.  If `easy_install` is completely deprecated, `setuptools` should call out to `pip` instead; or `easy_install` do what pip does to make TLS 1.2 work.

See also pypa/packaging-problems#134.

issue_title: Shared content slug must be unique across sites when `is_cross_site` is enabled
issue_body: The slug field is not checked for uniqueness across all sites when the `is_cross_site` is enabled. This causes the `MultipleObjectsReturned` errors when displaying the object.

issue_title: How do you specify a different encoding?
issue_body:
I want to feed some Big5-UAO encoded data. Since there is no `encoding` parameter (or something like that), I tried using `ByteStream`:
```py
stream = ByteStream(screen)
stream.select_other_charset("@")
stream.feed(bytes_object)
```
However, after checking the source code, it seems that this setup equals to:
```py
stream = Stream(screen)
stream.feed(bytes_object.decode("latin-1"))
```
This method doesn't work because the bytes of Big5-UAO encoded string may contain control characters like `\x9d`, and `match_text` failed to match the entire string:
https://github.com/selectel/pyte/blob/676610b43954b644c05823371df6daf87caafdad/pyte/streams.py#L132-L135
Here I generated a list of unicode character which contains control characters if encoded in Big5-UAO:
https://gist.github.com/eight04/3de731b7300a6b5036e082f801e2e3e9

#### How about encoding the bytes into unicode string with Big5-UAO before passing it to `stream.feed`?

We can't. In our usecase, we need a special feature called "雙色字". It colors a double width charater with two different colors. For example:

* Encode `"我"` into bytes `b'\xa7\xda'`
* Insert ANSI escape code to pos 0 and pos 1: `b'\x1b[1;31m\xa7\x1b[32m\xda'`
* This is what it looks like: ![https://i.imgur.com/j0hwhZM.png](https://i.imgur.com/j0hwhZM.png)

As a result, we can't decode the bytes before the escape code is parsed.

-------

May we can add a flag to disable C1 controls in `Stream.feed` parser?

issue_title: Managing definitions describing individual regions as in CRTF region files.
issue_body:
Currently, the region classes has `meta` as well as `visual` attributes for describing them.
but there are no proper documentations for them . The possible keys and their values for the `meta` dictionary that are being already supported by DS9 region format is unclear.
The CRTF format has a long list of global parameters for each region/annotations.
The problem that appears when it is handled in the `meta` dictionary are:
1. The key can have different names and there is no way to check what user assigns.Ex: both `meta['veltype']='RADIO'` and `meta['velocitytype']='RADIO'` are possible which may lead to confusion.
2. There are specific possible values for  the key  that is valid but there is no way to check that.
3. The same key supported by both DS9 and CRTF formats may have different formats to store the same value.

The question is whether they should be handled as keys in the `meta` attribute or should be wrapped around a separate object or may be just create a new function to validate?
This applies for the `visual` attribute also.

I've turned off that these are raised as errors, but obviously if someone knows how to fix them quickly it may be better. However I think it's rather low priority given #54 will get rid of this dependency.

issue_title: Provide a nodata parameter to measure.find_contours
issue_body:
## Provide a nodata parameter to measure.find_contours
At the moment, contours found by `skimage.measure.find_contours` which intersect the array edge are left open and others are closed ([docs](http://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.measure.html#skimage.measure.find_contours)).

Where the data of interest is masked but the array can't be cropped to this data (e.g. the area of interest is rotated or an unusual shape) and surrounding 'pixels' are set to NaN, contours are closed where they meet the NaNs. There's an [illustration in this question](https://gis.stackexchange.com/questions/278516/how-could-i-avoid-extracting-incorrectly-closed-contours-from-a-raster-with-angl) on Stack Exchange.

Could a `nodata` parameter (e.g. `nodata=np.NaN` or `nodata=-9999`) be provided to `measure.find_contours` which treats the `nodata` value in the same way as the edge of the array, returning an open contour when it leaves the data of interest and meets a `nodata` pixel?

I was playing with scikit functions and noticed, that in **color** module there are two same function **rgb2gray** and **rgb2grey** with similar description and functionality. Is it made just for convenience to write gray\grey?)

PS. in v0.13dev these functions exist as well.

In my current installation (scikit-image 0.11.3) I get:
`UnboundLocalError: local variable 'min_mask' referenced before assignment` in line 208 of future/graph/graph_cut.py (function `get_min_ncut`).
This only can happen if `cost` keeps the value `np.inf`. `min_mask` will never be assigned before it is returned.

To fix this `min_mask` should be initialized properly at the beginning of the loop.

issue_body: Currently, you have to extract the right info from the network and calculate the raw anomaly score manually. There should be a region that performs this computation.

Related to #2095

## Task

This task is to move the Python tests into `nupic/tests/` so that they are importable. This will make sharing test code much easier. Here is an article advocating this approach:

http://the-hitchhikers-guide-to-packaging.readthedocs.org/en/latest/creation.html

The code would look something like this:

```
README.md
setup.py
nupic
|- __init__.py
|- algorithms
|- ...
|- tests
  |- __init__.py
  |- test_algorithms.py
```

## Background

Current the tests are in a top level `tests` directory that is not on the PYTHONPATH. This makes it difficult for tests to share code. The `nupic.test` package is used to share test code, creating a separate of test scripts in `tests/` and test helpers in `nupic.test`. And some test discovery tools add test directories not on the path to the path, which can cause name conflicts. This will fail, for instance:

```
cd tests/unit
py.test .
```

Some tests will fail since the `nupic` package in `tests/unit` is confused for the top-level `nupic` Python package.

issue_title: WebSearch: accelerate nearest terms display for not-found phrases
issue_body:
Observed with @Kennethhole on some @TIND sites.

Searching for a Boolean query that does not find anything (say `first meeting at foo and bar`) is usually fast. (~0.1 seconds)

Searching for phrase query (`"first meeting at foo and bar"`) can be however very slow, e.g. ~0.1 seconds in collection index, ~5 seconds in title index, ~15 in global index.  (depending on the number of terms each given index has)

There is a solution: the admin can switch off the display of nearest terms altogether by setting `CFG_WEBSEARCH_DISPLAY_NEAREST_TERMS` to 0.

However, if the site needs to display nearest terms for the end users, then this facility must be speeded up:

- profiling (`search?p=...&verbose=9&profile=t`) shows that the bottleneck is `get_nearest_terms_in_idxphrase()`
- a possible solution is to dispatch user to `get_nearest_terms_in_bibwords()` if the index length is greater than ~50,000 terms  
  - the sweet spot should be find and made configurable via a new CFG variable  
  - the `get_nearest_terms_in_idxphrase()` query could first check the number of rows of e.g. `idxPHRASE08F` table and if the rows are less than ~50,000 then go for it, otherwise dispatch to `get_nearest_terms_in_bibwords()`.

@audub Can you please take care of this?

(this issue was previously there: inveniosoftware/invenio-demosite#63)

SCENARIO:
- user creates a new submission and fill mandatory fields
- user clicks on `choose files` and choose a file
- user click on `submit`

RESULT:
The new created submission does not have any document even though the user chose a document.

PROPOSED SOLUTIONS:
1. Display a popup warning if the user chose a file without uploading it.
2. Always upload documents when clicking on submit. We could then remove the `start upload` button.
3. Slight design improvements: the progress bar is not explicit. A user does not understand that it is related to upload. It only seems to be a grey rectangle. We can add a 0% or "not uploaded" inside. The `start upload` button could also be more visible.

Another problem is the `save` button which saves everything except the files which have not been uploaded.

As of yet the entry point discovery is only possible for [flask_admin.contrib.sqla.ModelView](https://flask-admin.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/mod_contrib_sqla/#flask_admin.contrib.sqla.ModelView). It should be also possible to define and add [flask_admin.base.BaseView](https://flask-admin.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/mod_base/#flask_admin.base.BaseView)

The entry-point interface doesn't need to change, since the loaded object is a dictionary containing keys 'model' and 'modelview' plus some extras ('category' etc.). To register a BaseView, the InvenioAdmin's init code should check for existence of key 'model' and call the corresponding constructor.

issue_title: es5: specific templates for each ES major version
issue_body:
Determine the structure and workings of how templates will be loaded for different major ES versions:

```
# For ES2
templates/record-stats
(or templates/v2/record-stats)

# For >ES2
templates/v5/record-stats
templates/v6/record-stats
...
```

(_This is partially addressed in #103_)

The [recursive functions that walk/parse templates/mappings](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-search/blob/287e284b417964b3c8b003073f49820683be9940/invenio_search/ext.py#L99-L129) are a [usual source of confusion](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-search/pull/145#discussion_r199137874) and are hard to tweak when changes or new features are introduced (e.g. supporting multiple ES versions, index/alias prefixing, etc). 

In the end how most people would think about this problem would be something like:

1. Build a tree with all mappings/templates
2. Create the mappings/templates from the tree leaves
3. Create aliases based on tree roots and their leaves

Of course any refactoring should include tests to prevent breaking existing behaviour.

oauth2server: user identity is not loaded when token is used
issue_body:
**Problem:**
The flask-principal `Identity` is not set to the token's user when a token is used to access HTTP views.

**Suggested solution:**
When token are used, [`invenio_oauth2server.views.server.login_oauth2_user`](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-oauth2server/blob/8d7c81babb8fe22d8bd3e85b23870edd89eaa9a7/invenio_oauth2server/views/server.py#L53) sets the `current_user` but does not set the flask-principal `Identity`.

This can be fixed by also sending the `identity_changed` signal in `invenio_oauth2server.views.server.login_oauth2_user`.

**Simple test case:**

```python
from __future__ import absolute_import, print_function

import os

from flask import Flask, url_for, request, abort
from flask_login import current_user
from flask_babelex import Babel
from flask_breadcrumbs import Breadcrumbs
from flask_cli import FlaskCLI
from flask_mail import Mail
from flask_menu import Menu
from flask_oauthlib.provider import OAuth2Provider
from invenio_accounts import InvenioAccounts
from invenio_access import InvenioAccess
from invenio_accounts.views import blueprint as accounts_blueprint
from invenio_db import InvenioDB, db

from invenio_oauth2server import InvenioOAuth2Server, current_oauth2server, \
    require_api_auth, require_oauth_scopes, InvenioOAuth2ServerREST
from invenio_oauth2server.models import Scope, Token
from invenio_oauth2server.views import server_blueprint, settings_blueprint
from invenio_oauth2server.provider import oauth2
from flask_security.utils import encrypt_password
from invenio_db import db
from sqlalchemy_utils.functions import create_database, drop_database
from flask_principal import Permission, identity_loaded, identity_changed, \
    Identity

# Create Flask application
app = Flask(__name__)
app.config.update(
    SERVER_NAME='localhost:5000',
    CELERY_ALWAYS_EAGER=True,
    CELERY_CACHE_BACKEND="memory",
    CELERY_EAGER_PROPAGATES_EXCEPTIONS=True,
    CELERY_RESULT_BACKEND="cache",
    OAUTH2SERVER_CACHE_TYPE='simple',
    OAUTHLIB_INSECURE_TRANSPORT=True,
    TESTING=True,
    SECRET_KEY='test_key',
    LOGIN_DISABLED=False,
    SQLALCHEMY_TRACK_MODIFICATIONS=True,
    SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI=os.getenv('SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI',
                                      'sqlite:///example.db'),
)
FlaskCLI(app)
Babel(app)
Mail(app)
Menu(app)
Breadcrumbs(app)
InvenioDB(app)
OAuth2Provider(app)

accounts = InvenioAccounts(app)
app.register_blueprint(accounts_blueprint)
access = InvenioAccess(app)

InvenioOAuth2Server(app)
InvenioOAuth2ServerREST(app)

# register blueprints
app.register_blueprint(settings_blueprint)
app.register_blueprint(server_blueprint)

result = {'loaded': False}

# print on identity change
def on_identity_loaded(sender, identity):
    result['loaded'] = True
    print('IDENTITY LOADED: identity={}'.format(identity))

identity_loaded.connect_via(app)(on_identity_loaded)

@app.route('/', methods=['GET'])
@require_api_auth()
@require_oauth_scopes('test:scope')
def example():
    """Index."""
    # Uncomment this to make the test pass
    # identity_changed.send(app,
    #                       identity=Identity(current_user.id))
    return "hello world"


with app.app_context():
    create_database(db.engine.url)
    db.create_all()
    # Register a test scope
    current_oauth2server.register_scope(Scope('test:scope'))


    creator = accounts.datastore.create_user(
        email='reader@inveniosoftware.org',
        password=encrypt_password('123456'),
        active=True,
    )
    db.session.commit()

    token = Token.create_personal(
        'mytoken', creator.id,
        scopes=['test:scope']
    )
    db.session.commit()

    with app.test_client() as client:
        headers = [('Accept', 'application/json'),
                   ('Authorization',
                    'Bearer {}'.format(token.access_token))
                   ]
        request_res = client.get(
            url_for('example'),
            headers=headers)
        assert request_res.status_code == 200
        assert result['loaded'] == True

```

I have the following piece of code:

    import numexpr as ne

        prev = d

I'm essentially taking the difference of two random vectors and finding that after repeating that for some time the difference changes significantly.

This code runs fine with numexpr-2.4.3, I'm using Anaconda on Windows AMD64 with the newest version of NumPy (1.10, 1.9 showed the same problem though), and the problem only appears when running multithreaded. Can someone else reproduce this?

It seems like a threading issue that leads to parts of the difference array being values very close to zero (~1e-315).

issue_title: Update Long+Short+Cash plot y-values to match Gross Leverage plot
issue_body: might make more sense visually is have the long+short+cash plot y-axis values match up with the gross exposure y-axis

I ran into a lot of trouble with this, so I'm sure other people must have, too.  I can't tell if it's a documentation problem, or a code problem, or both.  I was able to fix my setup with just changes to my code and configuration, though.

Background: I need to use a synchronous transport (because my application's process will exit shortly after an exception that I want to report to Sentry), so when I heard that Sentry used an async transport by default, I went and read this document: http://raven.readthedocs.org/en/latest/transports/index.html

First of all, the sentence "By default, Raven uses a threaded asynchronous transport, but you can easily adjust this by modifying your SENTRY_DSN value." is a little confusing.  It makes it sound like the DSN is what selects the transport.  But then two lines down when it shows some sample code, the DSN doesn't say what transport to use.  The "transport=" keyword parameter does.  But maybe the strange wording here was intentional.  I'll come back to this below.

When I try something like this:

    import raven
    s = raven.Client(dsn='https://FOO:BAR@app.getsentry.com/BAZ',
                     transport=raven.transport.HTTPTransport)
    try:
        1/0
    except:
        s.captureException()

it doesn't show up in the Sentry dashboard.  Similarly for raver.transport.RequestsHTTPTransport.  (It returns an event_id without error, but after a bit of searching I discovered that the event_id is created on the client-side, so this was not an indication of success.)

Upon digging through the logs, I discovered that it was failing here: https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/transport/base.py#L29

It turns out that each Transport class has its own "scheme" list, and it will refuse to even try to send an event unless your store_endpoint has a protocol in that list.  ThreadedHTTPTransport (the default) has ['http', 'https', 'threaded+http', 'threaded+https'].  HTTPTransport's is ['sync+http', 'sync+https'], and RequestsHTTPTransport's is ['requests+http', 'requests+https'].  Internally, it builds the store_endpoint from the DSN you provide, with the same protocol.

This, combined with the strange sentence at the top (which suggested that the DSN needed modification in order to pick a different Transport) led me to figure out how to get it working with other transports.  For example, to use the RequestsHTTPTransport, I had to:

 1. pass transport=raven.transport.RequestsHTTPTransport to the Client initializer, and also
 2. change my DSN from "https" (as getsentry.com had told me) to "requests+https".

Then everything works!  But this was not at all easy to figure out.

By the way, this also means that the sample code on page <http://raven.readthedocs.org/en/latest/transports/index.html> won't work.  It asks for transport=HTTPTransport, but the DSN URL starts with "http", when it should start with "sync+http".

Alternatively, I suppose, you could just remove check_scheme.  I'm not sure why it exists.

issue_title: Exception being send to Sentry even when add to ignore_exception
issue_body:
Hello,

I have a Django project using raven 5.27.1 and I setting the the `ignore_exception` like so:

```python
RAVEN_CONFIG = {
    ...
    'ignore_exceptions': [myproject.base.exceptions.BackOffException'],
    ...
}
```

I also have a celery task that raises that exception:

```python
@shared_task(throws=(BackOffException,))
def my_useful_task():
     raise BackOffException
```

My problem is that raven is not respecting my `ignore_exception` config and sending the exception to Sentry anyway. The problem seems to be that the [should_capture](https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/base.py#L786) is being executed twice, I added the following to log to it:


```python
    def should_capture(self, exc_info):
        exc_type = exc_info[0]
        exc_name = '%s.%s' % (exc_type.__module__, exc_type.__name__)
        exclusions = self.ignore_exceptions
        self.logger.error('hello {} {}'.format(exc_name, exclusions))
```

And these are logs that I am seeing:

```
hello myproject.base.exceptions.BackOffException set()

hello myproject.base.exceptions.BackOffException {'myproject.base.exceptions.BackOffException'}

Not capturing exception due to filters: <class 'myproject.base.exceptions.BackOffException'>

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/code/virtualenv/CURRENT/lib/python3.5/site-packages/celery/app/trace.py", line 240, in trace_task
    R = retval = fun(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/code/virtualenv/CURRENT/lib/python3.5/site-packages/celery/app/trace.py", line 438, in __protected_call__
    return self.run(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/code/scp/python/myproject/tasks.py", line 98, in my_useful_task
    raise BackOffException
myproject.base.exceptions.BackOffException
pathname=/code/virtualenv/CURRENT/lib/python3.5/site-packages/celery/utils/log.py lineno=282 Task myproject.my_useful_task[145d2141-8fbc-4c9e-bcad-4ce8c3d9b7d9] raised unexpected: BackOffException()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/code/virtualenv/CURRENT/lib/python3.5/site-packages/celery/app/trace.py", line 240, in trace_task
    R = retval = fun(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/code/virtualenv/CURRENT/lib/python3.5/site-packages/celery/app/trace.py", line 438, in __protected_call__
    return self.run(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/code/scp/python/myproject/tasks.py", line 98, in my_useful_task
    raise BackOffException
myproject.base.exceptions.BackOffException
```

As you can see, `capture_exception` was called twice, the first time the exclusion list was an empty set. I think this is causing the error to be sent to Sentry anyway

issue_title: Allow anonymous connections to the S3 cache backend
issue_body:
S3 has the ability to set ACLs for anonymous access to buckets. This is wired into boto's [s3.connection.S3Connection method](http://boto.readthedocs.org/en/latest/ref/s3.html#module-boto.s3.connection) via the anon parameter.

Modify the S3 cache backend code so that if the user does not have credentials, it will connect as an anonymous user rather than erroring with "No handler was ready to authenticate... Check your credentials" and disabling the S3CacheBackend.

issue_title: Implement ENV secrets for PostgreSQL user accounts
issue_body:
We need a better way to set passwords for the three PostgreSQL accounts we need to use than keeping them in a configuration file which may be in poorly secured CMS repo.   I suggest that we use envdir, as several other Python projects (like WAL-E) do.  

Other ideas?

Hello,

A common use case which I currently implement often is as follows:

Site A:
 - node A1
 - node A2
 - node A3

Site D (DR site):
 - node D1
 - node D2
 - node D3

Site A would be configured as a normal Patroni cluster.

Site D would be configured as a master-less cluster with D1 attached to A(master) and D2 and D3 cascading to D1.

Ideally I would actually have D2 and D3 replicating from D(lock owner which wasn't a master) - but I don't have this today.

Is there a way to do this in Patroni, or can anyone suggest somewhere I could concentrate some effort to make it work?

The requirements are:

- Two 'sites' A/D
- A is fully protected by automatic failover within site, but not between sites (manual failover to D only)
- D has no master under normal operation, with one node connected to A and the other two nodes cascading
- Failover to D is a single command, which brings up a node as master (and removes the cascading links if needed)

Cheers,
James

A feature request I got: when restoring a cluster from backup one has to make sure all Patroni instances are shut down before replacing one data dir from backup, doing the whole recovery dance and possibly adjusting `/optime/leader` and then bringing the nodes back up in correct order. Would be nice to have some tooling for that to reduce room for operator errors and speed things up. Ideally make a restore from backup scriptable.

Something along the lines of:

1. patronictl backup-recovery init - shut down postgresql on all nodes.
2a. patronictl backup-recovery complete - start postgres, make it the leader, update /optime/leader, reinit standby's.
2b. patronictl backup-recovery abort - start rest of the cluster, reinit current node.

I'll leave this as a place holder until the idea has had some time to develop.

### Description

TL;TR: Move Connexion's configurations to the config attribute of the Flask object.

Currently we have over 10 parameters for the constructor of `connexion.app.App`, `connexion.api.API` classes and `connexion.api.App.add_api`. Most of those parameters are configurations that can be set for a specific API definition or globally in the Connexion server instance. This set of configurations are passed along one-by-one and it was OK for a small set of possible configurations, but now we are constantly increasing the possibilities of how to configure Connexion and adding new options to configure. To permit a more flexible and clean way to move forward whether when adding more features or maintaining the current code base we need to change how we deal with configuration options. Flask already has [a built-in](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.11/config/) configuration handling module and we could make use of that for Connexion.

When specifying {{https}} as the scheme in the API YAML file, all the URIs in the Swagger UI that is served are HTTPS endpoints. The problem is that the default server that is running is a "normal" HTTP server. This means that the Swagger UI cannot be used to play with the API.

What is the correct way to start a HTTPS server using connexion?

```
from gcloud import datastore

from gcloud import datastore

def onTaskPushed( future ):
	message_id = future.result()
	Log.info( 'task sent. ' + message_id )

def sendTask( userId ):
	Log.info('start send task with user ' + userId)

	project_id = get_project_id() # get project id
	publisher = pubsub_v1.PublisherClient()
	topic_path = publisher.topic_path(project_id, 'tasks')

	data = {}
	data['user_id'] = userId
	data['time'] = Time.time() # get unix time in second
	data = json.dumps( data )

	data = data.encode( 'utf-8' )
	future = publisher.publish(topic_path, data=data )
	future.add_done_callback(onTaskPushed)

issue_title: Error Reporting: Docs for HTTPContext incorrect
issue_body:
The [documentation](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/error-reporting/usage.html) for `error_reporting.client.HTTPContext` gives the wrong parameter names.

```python
>>> from google.cloud import error_reporting
>>> client = error_reporting.Client()
>>> user = 'example@gmail.com'
>>> http_context = HTTPContext(method='GET', url='/', userAgent='test agent',
...                            referrer='example.com', responseStatusCode=500,
...                            remote_ip='1.2.3.4')
>>> try:
>>>     raise NameError
>>> except Exception:
>>>     client.report_exception(http_context=http_context, user=user))
```

Reading the code, it's clear that the camel-cased names are internally used to match expected JSON keys for the underlying API call, but the [actual parameter names are properly snake-cased](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/error_reporting/google/cloud/error_reporting/client.py#L64-L74). Oddly, the docs _do_ show the correct parameter name for `remote_ip`, but not `user_agent` or `response_status_code`.

I couldn't figure out which file to edit to fix, otherwise I would have just submitted a PR.

issue_title: Treat 3rdparty as a 1st class distinction amongst source roots.
issue_body: As noted [here](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/cd0ce6f5ae50f10ebdb7ca024a3e4bd4c840e08c/src/python/pants/source/source_root.py#L155-L155), the source root system only distinguishes between source and test roots today and this forces Go plugin users to manually define source roots for one.  Elevating  the distinction of a root that contains remote dependency information as first class should allow handling language integration like the Go contrib plugin that care about this distinction.

issue_title: Add some randomness when compiling intermediate deps when there are multiple candidates to build
issue_body:
We have a CI builder that launches builds in parallel.  Some of these CI workers are sharded builds of the same set of tests.  When there  are lots of stale dependencies to build, they all execute the build in the same order, and encounter the same stale deps at the same time.  When pants encounters this dependency relationship:

a -> B 
a -> C
a -> D

It would save overall build time if some of the shards build B, others built C first, and others built D.  Right now, everyone pounces on 'B' and then everyone pounces on 'C', then everyone tries 'D'.

When testing v2 engine in master, I found that LegacyAddressMapper does not have scan_build_files method, which is used by a few tasks. To make all integration tests in master pass on v2 engine, we have to extend the interface coverage of LegacyAddressMapper.

As part of the effort, LegacyAddressMapper should be v2 engine backed (similar to LegacyBuildGraph), which means the new scan_build_files method, along with all existing methods, should be implemented using v2 engine directly.

issue_title: Mark nodes "dirty" during invalidation, rather than deleting them
issue_body:
After a change event and during invalidation, we transitively delete `Nodes` from the graph. But rather than deleting, we should mark `Nodes` "dirty" instead.

As describe in the `bazel`/`skyframe` design, and in the `adapton` design, marking a `Node` dirty allows it to be "resurrected" without re-running if all of its inputs are subsequently marked clean with the same values they had before.

This is an extremely efficient cache of the previous execution, because it can happen purely in memory.

issue_title: Profile `./pants list` overhead with the daemon
issue_body:
For the completely warm case with the daemon, very little time is spent in the engine (since all products have already been computed) and the daemon, and this is confirmed by the daemon's log. But a lot of time is currently unaccounted-for, and it would be good to have an understanding of where the time is going for (in case it is something architecturally important).

As an example, the daemon's log for a warm run of `./pants list 3rdparty::` in Twitter's source repo looks like:
```
I1005 13:36:11.372629 9950 pailgun_server.py:72] handling pailgun request: `./pants list 3rdparty::`
D1005 13:36:11.373440 9950 pailgun_server.py:73] pailgun request environment: <snip!>

D1005 13:36:11.531510 9950 git.py:292] Executing: git --git-dir=/Users/stuhood/src/source/.git --work-tree=/Users/stuhood/src/source merge-base origin/master HEAD
D1005 13:36:11.639550 9950 target_roots.py:49] args are: [u'./pants', u'list', u'3rdparty::']

D1005 13:36:11.640206 9950 target_roots.py:51] changed_request is: ChangedRequest(changes_since=None, diffspec=None, include_dependees='none', fast=False)

D1005 13:36:11.642249 9950 process_manager.py:194] purging metadata directory: /Users/stuhood/src/source/.pids/pantsd-run-2016-10-05T13_36_11_641971
D1005 13:36:11.642498 9950 process_manager.py:425] forking <pants.bin.daemon_pants_runner.DaemonPantsRunner object at 0x105726390>
```

The log (assuming we can trust it) indicates that we are spending a total of `0.270` milliseconds in the daemon (`642-372`) and `0.000011` milliseconds in the engine.

But on the frontend for the same run, there is a lot of time un-accounted for:
```

<snip>
    1660

real	0m2.526s
user	0m0.283s
sys	0m0.185s
```

----

One issue is obvious in the frontend with the `-ldebug` flag: some work is being re-executed post-fork (likely due to https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/4168/ cc @kwlzn), but it still only accounts for a fraction of the total runtime.

```

In OSS pants, 
`$ for i in `seq 10`; do time ./pants goals &> /dev/null; done` ranges from 1.2 to 1.5 seconds. 

In twitter, we consume pants in pex. sample data:
`$ for i in `seq 10`; do time ./pants goals &> /dev/null; done` ranges from 2.3 to 3 seconds. which is way too much for a simple operations like `./options` or `goals`.

The overhead not only comes from pants itself, but from unpacking pants from pex as well.

Hence I am proposing/ going to experiment the idea to spin a lightweight server that listens to incoming commands right before bootstrapping options (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/1f554c8ca1fc56b4830091d7c75aac3056785e9e/src/python/pants/bin/pants_runner.py#L49-L49)

Comments / suggestions welcome!

issue_title: Not parsing valid address: 12 LITTLE WEST 12TH STR, New York, NY
issue_body:
Hi I am getting a RepeatedLabelError: when running `usaddress.tag('12 LITTLE WEST 12TH STR')`

Here is the error:
`ERROR: Unable to tag this string because more than one area of the string has the same label`
 `ORIGINAL STRING:  12 LITTLE WEST 12TH STR
PARSED TOKENS:    [(u'12', 'AddressNumber'), (u'LITTLE', 'StreetName'), (u'WEST', 'StreetNamePreDirectional'), (u'12TH', 'StreetName'), (u'STR', 'StreetNamePostType')]
UNCERTAIN LABEL:  StreetName`

12 LITTLE WEST 12TH STR. It is a valid address in New York City; `LITTLE WEST 12TH` is the street name and first `12` is the street number. I don't have any other similar addresses in my dataset to submit for training, but a table of addresses can be generated with like-so addresses:

Ex: 
12 LITTLE WEST 18TH STR
20 LITTLE WEST 100TH STR
etc.

They all  get same error.

Currently, we just list the different available address tags, but don't provide definitions. For users to translate these tags to their own data model, these definitions would be very useful.


    AddressNumber
    StreetName
    PlaceName
    StateName
    ZipCode
    AddressNumberPrefix
    AddressNumberSuffix
    StreetNamePreDirectional
    StreetNamePostDirectional

issue_title: Stop the event listener if no subscriptions are active
issue_body:
When the user subscribes to a service, the event server is started. When he unsubscribes again, the event server keeps running until the user calls `event_listener.stop()`, or as long as the interpreter runs.
If the user calls `event_listener.stop()`, there might still be other code which has a running subscription, which is then killed. This wouldn't be necessary if we'd just automatically stop the listener when the last subscription is cancelled (if `_subscriptions` is empty on unsubscribe).

issue_title: AttributeError on parse error for layer.yaml with req.txt version of ruamel.yaml
issue_body:
## Checklist

 - [X] Confirmed this is an issue with charm-tools, not charmstore-client
 - [X] Provide versions of tools used
 - [X] Described the feature or ways to replicate the issue

## What version am I running?

I ran the following command: `charm version` and got the following ouput:

```
charm error
charm-tools 2.1.3
```

I am using: Ubuntu trusty

## Issue/Feature

### I expect/expected the following

With the version of `ruamel.yaml` that is pinned in `requirements.txt` (0.10.23) if there is a YAML syntax error in `layer.yaml` the error handler designed to catch it instead fails with an `AttributeError` due to `ruamel.yaml.parser` having been temporarily renamed to `ruamel.yaml.parser_`.  This has been resolved in newer versions of `ruamel.yaml`.

Additionally, we don't have `ruamel.yaml` pinned in `setup.py` so most people won't hit this.  However, #229 shows that breaking changes get introduced to `ruamel.yaml` frequently enough that we *should* pin it.

I propose that we update `requirements.txt` to the current, working version (0.11.14) and pin it to that in `setup.py`.  We should probably also report the parsing error in a more friendly way, although the current error does provide a sufficiently useful message (if not with the prettiest presentation).

## Description
After pip installing charm-helpers, my system's dist-packages directory has a 'tools' and 'tests' directory from charmhelpers. In doing so other python3 software that is trying to import "tests" or "tools" fails to execute because of this.

## Expected Results
No `tests` or `tools` directories from pycharms in my dist-packages directory.

## Actual Results
A `tests` and `tools` directory that override various namespaces, preventing the importing of those same directories by other tools.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. `lxc launch ubuntu-daily:x x`
2. `lxc exec x bash`
3. `apt update`
4. `apt install -y python3-pip`
5. `pip install charm-helpers`
6. Notice `tests` and `tools` directory under `/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages`

Example: https://paste.ubuntu.com/26485423/

Hi,

The `@cached` mechanism in `core/hookenv.py` is broken. The key for the `cache` dict is created as follows (from https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/master/charmhelpers/core/hookenv.py#L70) : 

```
key = str((func, args, kwargs))
```

But there's a subtle difference in how `str()` called on a dict (`kwargs`, here) behaves between xenial and bionic.

On xenial : 
```
 >>> a = {'rid': 'general-info:2', 'unit': 'ubuntu/0'}
>>> str(a)
"{'rid': 'general-info:2', 'unit': 'ubuntu/0'}"
>>> b = {'unit': 'ubuntu/0', 'rid': 'general-info:2'}
>>> str(b)
"{'rid': 'general-info:2', 'unit': 'ubuntu/0'}"
>>> str(a) == str(b)
True
```

On bionic : 
```
>>> a = {'rid': 'general-info:2', 'unit': 'ubuntu/0'}
>>> str(a)
"{'rid': 'general-info:2', 'unit': 'ubuntu/0'}"
>>> b = {'unit': 'ubuntu/0', 'rid': 'general-info:2'}
>>> str(b)
"{'unit': 'ubuntu/0', 'rid': 'general-info:2'}"
>>> str(a) == str(b)
False
```

In other words, on xenial, the orders of the keys in the dict doesn't matter, but on bionic it does.

So for example, the `relation_get()` method being cached, if I call `relation_get(rid=foo, unit=bar)` and `relation_get(unit=bar, rid=foo)`, on xenial they will have the same cache key, but on bionic they won't.

This shouldn't result in bugs however, just in slower calls. There is a twist however : some functions modify the object returned by the cached function call, which modify the object in the cache itself.

For example : 
```
@cached
def relation_for_unit(unit=None, rid=None):
    """Get the json represenation of a unit's relation"""
    unit = unit or remote_unit()
    relation = relation_get(unit=unit, rid=rid)
    for key in relation:
        if key.endswith('-list'):
            relation[key] = relation[key].split()
    relation['__unit__'] = unit
return relation
```
(taken from https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/master/charmhelpers/core/hookenv.py#L466)

This will add a `__unit___` key to the dict that is stored in the cache.

Now consider the following call, taken from https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/master/charmhelpers/core/services/helpers.py#L119 :
```
reldata = hookenv.relation_get(rid=rid, unit=unit)
```

On xenial, because `str(a) == str(b)` (see the first block of code), this call and `relation_get(unit=unit, rid=rid)` (kwargs in different order) will have the same key in the cache, and so will use the same cached object, containing `__unit__`. However on bionic, because `str(a)!=str(b)`, these 2 calls will not have the same key, and so will not use the same object from the cache. So the `reldata` object above will _never_ have a `__unit__` key in it.

The short term fix I'm going to propose here is to have all calls to `relation_get()` in charmhelpers have the same order for kwargs.

For the long term, this caching technique must be changed. Perhaps using https://docs.python.org/dev/library/functools.html#functools.lru_cache ? I'll leave that up to you.

issue_title: % display contents crashes the web app if the path is wrong
issue_body:
I just spent a while panicking because it looked for a moment like the app ate a rather large worksheet, and it turns out I'd just mistyped a path in a `% display contents` directive. I've been able to reproduce the error to figure out the consistent behavior described below. Literally any other response to this type of operator error would be preferable :)

1. Edit the worksheet to include a display contents directive. Type garbage in for the path. Follow with a valid UUID.
1. Hit ^ENTER or the save button. It appears to save as usual.
1. Hit 'e' to re-enter the editing interface. This has no effect.
1. Hit the "Edit Source" button. This has no effect.
1. Interface appears frozen. Reload the page.
1. Worksheet title reads <none> and body reads (empty)
1. The only buttons available are "View" and "View Source"
1. "View Source" shows a source view of the original content. Hooray, it's not gone! We also see the message "Press ctrl-enter to save. See markdown syntax." even though we're not in the editor. Buttons are "Save" and "Discard Changes".
1. Click "Save". Interface enters edit mode, and the "Updating..." widget spins indefinitely. 
1. Further clicks on "Save" have no effect. "Discard Changes" returns to source view mode, but "Updating..." perseveres.
1. 'e' has no effect.
1. ^ENTER enters edit mode.
1. Reloading the page returns to <none> / (empty)

When I load the bad sheet with the javascript console open, I get the error "this.props.item.interpreted is null ...  contents_item_interface.js:21:13"

This clued me in that it was an issue with the contents directive, and I was able to edit and binary-search my way through the document using the command-line editor on my local machine... but there's gotta be a more graceful way to handle the issue in the frontend.

The Python MangoPay SDK ignores MangoPay API pagination information specified in the documentation: https://docs.mangopay.com/guide/lists-pagination-management

For now, the only thing offered by the library is to retrieve a paginated list with a given page number and a number of items per page, such as:

```
>>> users = User.all(page=1, per_page=10)
[ { ... }, ... ]
```

However there is no way to get the total number of items and the total number of pages.
The library should make available at least these 2 informations for the requested list.
The following MangoPay API response headers contains those informations:

`X-Number-Of-Pages: 2`
`X-Number-Of-Items: 12`

At the moment, lacking those 2 informations makes the pagination support almost unusable (or maybe I missed something, if so please let me know).

Unfortunately, I'm a bit short of proposal but at first glance there is no quick-fix.
One potential way to integrate this would be return some metadata along the requested list, introducing a different `all()` method in order to maintain backward-compatibility with all `all()` method calls:

```
>>> users = User.all_paginated(page=1, per_page=10)
{
  num_items: 12,
  num_pages: 2,
  per_page: 10,
  page: 1,
  items: [ { ... }, ... ],
}
```

For reference, here are some other libraries takes on the matter:
- Flask-SQLAlchemy: http://flask-sqlalchemy.pocoo.org/2.3/api/#flask_sqlalchemy.Pagination
- Stripe: https://stripe.com/docs/api/python#pagination

What's your take on this?

issue_title: Add explicit error message if SNI fails
issue_body:
With Python 2.x, you get the error message "requests.exceptions.SSLError: hostname 'api.scaleway.com' doesn't match '*.cloud.online.net'" if the some packages, required by `python-requests`, aren't installed. This message is cryptic, and often misunderstood by the users who don't know what SNI is.

The packages required by `python-requests` to handle SNI are:
- pyasn1
- ndg-httpsclient
- pyOpenSSL

To compile them you need (on Ubuntu) to run `apt-get install python-dev libffi-dev libssl-dev` prior their installation.

There's a problem if you do the installation in a bad order. If you:

- run `pip install scaleway-sdk`
- something doesn't work, because you don't have the required packages, so you `apt-get install python-dev libffi-dev libssl-dev`
- then you try to re-run `pip install scaleway-sdk`

The packages won't be recompiled (and I don't really know why, I need to investigate).

If you're in this case, you need to `pip uninstall -v pyasn1 ndg-httpsclient pyOpenSSL scaleway-sdk` after installing the dependencies, and before running `pip install scaleway-sdk`.


Anyway, we need to:
- add an explicit message if requests.exceptions.SSLError is raised
- see why the Python packages required to handle SNI aren't reinstalled when you're trying to install sdk, install the dependencies and reinstall the sdk.

Hello, folks!

I tough many nights about flowspec and found very challenging problem. In most popular cases we are deploying flowspec with upstream for attack mitigation. But flowspec implemented only by Juniper. What should I do if I haven't Juniper equipment? 

Well, I could install exabgp and code for it. But in case of DDoS attack will be fine to get web interface with interactive interface for building rulesets according to Juniper specification and upstream restrictions (my upstream provide only 10 rules).

The second challenging task with flow spec is monitoring. AFAIK, there are no ways to check "how much traffic hit this rule" and every ISP should implement custom interface. Will be fine implement some standard approach to this task.

issue_title: Modify neighbor via API without requiring config reread
issue_body:
I can see a scenario happening where a BGP neighbor needs to be add/removed from ExaBGP's peering without affecting the other neighbors. Currently, to add/remove/shutdown a peer the ExaBGP process must be restart to reread the config file.

An API command similar to the existing commands can be added, something like:
    ```neighbor add 10.10.0.1 peer-as 65000 [local-as 65001 | local-address 10.0.0.1]```
    ```neighbor shutdown 10.10.0.1```
    ```neighbor resume 10.10.0.1```
    ```neighbor remove 10.10.0.1```

issue_title: Client.events() should be able to get all cli options (since, until and filter) as arguments
The exclusion patterns of .dockerignore are not respected
issue_body:
This is a follow-up to what I explained in issue #1117: the way #1455 implemented the function `utils.build.exclude_paths()`, in particular by slicing the patterns into an _include_ and an _exclude_ lists, is wrong, as the [script attached to that issue](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/issues/1117#issuecomment-286694944) shows.

I think that the only way to get the right behaviour is to consider the patterns as an ordered list of rules that must be applied sequentially on each item obtained by iterating the filesystem subtree, something like the function `valid_paths()` I proposed [here](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/issues/1117#issuecomment-268176859), that I'm (still) using as a fixup.

issue_title: WebSocketClientFactory's _batched_timer SHOULD use the passed event loop
issue_body:
Hey,

here's my second issue report in a week:

The `WebSocketClientFactory` can be passed an event loop:

https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/bba4ed8418e59a678012ecb466092bee250b906f/autobahn/asyncio/websocket.py#L288-L289

.... and `WebSocketClientFactory`'s `_batched_timer` **SHOULD** use it, which it doesn't now:
https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/bba4ed8418e59a678012ecb466092bee250b906f/autobahn/websocket/protocol.py#L3798-L3801

Why is this a problem? Because if someone doesn't have a main event loop, autoPings won't work. My application doesn't have a **main** event loop, it only has an event loop in a separate thread, which I initialize later, along with the websocket client.

As far as I've seen, `txaio` uses the main event loop, and the websocket client can't be told to use other than the main initialized one.

Correct me if I'm wrong, but now I have to have an asynchronous main event loop, just to have autopings working?

issue_title: prefetch_related() that uses a Prefetch with a queryset is not joining correctly
issue_body:
Here is a failing test that can be added to `versions_tests.tests.test_models.PrefetchingHistoricTests`:
```
    def test_foreign_key_prefetch_with_historic_version(self):
        self.modify_objects()
        historic_city = City.objects.as_of(self.time1).get(identity=self.c1.identity)

        # Test with a simple prefetch
        team = Team.objects.as_of(self.time1).filter(identity=self.t1.identity).prefetch_related('city')[0]
        self.assertIsNotNone(team.city)
        self.assertEquals(team.city.id, historic_city.id)

        # Test with a Prefetch object without a queryset.
        team = Team.objects.as_of(self.time1).filter(
            identity=self.t1.identity
        ).prefetch_related(Prefetch(
            'city',
        ))[0]
        self.assertIsNotNone(team.city)
        self.assertEquals(team.city.id, historic_city.id)

        # Test with a Prefetch object with a queryset.
        team = Team.objects.as_of(self.time1).filter(
            identity=self.t1.identity
        ).prefetch_related(Prefetch(
            'city',
            queryset=City.objects.as_of(self.time1)
        ))[0]
        # This is currently failing:
        self.assertIsNotNone(team.city)
        self.assertEquals(team.city.id, historic_city.id)
```

The second-to-last assertion fails.

Inspecting the generated query, I can see that it tries to select the related objects using ids.  It should however select using identities and an as_of clause.

I suspect that the `VersionedReverseSingleRelatedObjectDescriptor` needs to have a custom `get_prefetch_queryset()` implementation.

It probably makes sense to merge the Django-1.9-compatibility code before fixing this.

This means that if a test doesn't return anything at all when scraping an example file, the test would pass, so long as no exception was raised. This is bad.

We should use the `to_json` method to make matching json files for every example file, and then add that to the tests. (Or do similar.)

issue_title: Problem with icons and omitted underspecified variable
issue_body:
There is a problem with omitted underspecified variables i and icons that refer to them. The sentence **_"Impressed by the cat, Kim laughed."_** should produce the following EP:
`[ _impress_v_1_rel<0:9> LBL: h7 ARG0: e8 [ e SF: prop TENSE: untensed MOOD: indicative ] ARG1: x9 ARG2: i10 ]`
Instead, it omits ARG2, but the icons list subsequently refers to it:
`ICONS: < e8 topic i10 >`

The same with the 1214 version, where icons are handled differently:
`[ _impress_v_1_rel<0:9> LBL: h7 ARG0: e8 [ e SF: prop TENSE: untensed MOOD: indicative ] ARG1: x9 ARG2: i10 ]
[ parg_d_rel LBL: h7 ARG0: e22 [ e SF: prop ] ARG1: e8 ARG2: i10 ]`

Overall, this should probably be parsed differently anyway, with i10 refering to the variable representing the subject of the main clause instead.

PyDelphin has no trouble reading or writing compressed (.gz) files in [incr tsdb()] profiles, so a nice feature for the `delphin` command would be to compress a profile in-place. Perhaps this could be part of the `mkprof` subcommand. This could be done just by adding an `--in-place` switch to alternate with the otherwise required `--source`:

```
delphin mkprof DEST (--source PROFILE | --in-place)
                    [--apply=APL]... [--filter=CND]...
                    [--relations=FILE] [--full|--skeleton] [--gzip]
                    [-v...|-q]
```

Then to compress:

```
delphin mkprof path/to/profile --in-place --full --gzip
```

To decompress:

```
delphin mkprof path/to/profile --in-place --full
```

But this could also be simpler, e.g. something like:

```
delphin mkprof path/to/profile --compress
delphin mkprof path/to/profile --decompress
```

But then we'd need a new usage synposis and 2 new options, instead of just one new option.

issue_title: conda is broken if your home directory is read-only
issue_body:
Conda currently requires the user's home directory to be writable.

If the directory conda is installed into is writable (say a tmpfs) then you can get along way by using 
```shell
		./Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh -p $CONDA_DIR -b -f
		conda config --system --set always_yes yes
		conda config --system --set changeps1 no
		conda config --system --add envs_dirs $CONDA_DIR/envs
		conda config --system --add pkgs_dirs $CONDA_DIR/pkgs
```

However, this is foiled by the following line -> https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/7616b87ad87b80da16b8263011c9c708be98147c/conda/core/envs_manager.py#L18

```python
USER_ENVIRONMENTS_TXT_FILE = expand(join('~', '.conda', 'environments.txt'))
```

I'm not sure if this would even work on Windows?

issue_title: `conda.install.update_prefix` is modifying cached pkgs in Windows
issue_body:
Since files are hardlinks, it looks like conda's prefix replacement mechanism is breaking its own files.

File contents inside package:

```python
x = r'/opt/anaconda1anaconda2anaconda3\Scripts',
```

File contents after installing in 'env1':

```python
x = r'C:\Miniconda\envs\env1\Scripts',
```

File contents after installing in 'NOTENV1':

```python
x = r'C:\Miniconda\envs\env1\Scripts',
```

Note that the second install fails, because the first one modified the cached files in `C:\Miniconda\pkgs`.
Reading @asmeurer comments in #679, I agree that the correct behavior in this case would be to delete the file being modified and re-create it to avoid this issue.

issue_title: 4.1.* post-link fails on 32b Windows py3.5 from conda-forge
issue_body:
When trying on appveyor to build and minimally test (via `imports`) a [conda-forge feedstock](https://github.com/conda-forge/nb_conda_kernels-feedstock/pull/7#issuecomment-226194960) that has a post-link script (i know, i know, [strongly discouraged](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/building/build-scripts.html)), i am getting `DLL load failed: The specified module could not be found` for the package i just installed.

Here are the [work/builds](https://github.com/conda-forge/nb_conda_kernels-feedstock/pull/7#issuecomment-226194960).

I am seeing the following conditions:

```batch
REM no condarc!
conda clean -y --all
conda install -f "conda>=4.1.0" conda-build
conda build recipe -c conda-forge
```

I see:
```
C:\projects\nb-conda-kernels-feedstock>"C:\conda\envs\_test\python.exe" -m nb_conda_kernels.install --enable --prefix="C:\conda\envs\_test" 
C:\conda\envs\_test\python.exe: Error while finding spec for 'nb_conda_kernels.install' (<class 'ImportError'>: DLL load failed: The specified module could not be found.)
Error: post-link failed for: local::nb_conda_kernels-1.0.3-py35_0
```

If I repeat this with various bits twiddled (after much `conda clean`ing), here are my results:

| conda | -c conda-forge | result |
|:-----:|:--------------:|:------:|
| 4.0.8 | no             | win    |
| 4.0.8 | yes            | win    |
| 4.1.0 | yes            | fail   |
| 4.1.1 | yes            | fail   |
| 4.1.1 | no             | win    |

issue_title: pip install of version 0.9.1.4 fails on OS X 10.11.6 both with gcc and clang
issue_body:
The installation of version 0.9.1.4 via **pip** fails with both the **gcc** and OS X-native **clang** compilers. The default installation by

```
pip install pysam
```

crushes like it is described in #267. The installation using the **gcc** compiler

```
env CC=/usr/local/bin/gcc-6 pip install pysam
```

shows the following error message (as in #307):

```
gcc-6: error: unrecognized command line option '-rpath'
```

### Description

The current implementation does not work within copybooks (or programs that have the "important" parser parts within copybooks).

It looks like the navigation tree needs a ``DIVISION`` header, otherwise it only contains "No Data".

Here's my suggestion:

* If "No Data" would be produced check if a section is found instead. If it is ``WORKING-STORAGE``, ``LOCAL-STORAGE``, ``LINKAGE``, ``REPORT`` or ``SCREEN`` assume ``DATA DIVISION``, otherwise assume ``PROCEDURE DIVISION``.
* If no section is found check if you see a possible level number (first word is a numeric word without a dot following), if yes: assume ``DATA DIVISION`` and ``WORKING-STORAGE SECTION``.

This would fix the Navigation tree in most use-cases (the current "copybook problem" occurs likely with much production code).

issue_title: [Enhancement] Let the advanced user choose the full path to the runtime module loader (cobcrun)
issue_body:
As a follow up to #183 it would be good to allow the user to set the runtime module loader, too. This would go into the run tab of application preferences with the following entries (similar to compiler settings):

Runtime module loader path, additional command line options [have to be added directly after the module loader, before the COBOL module when OCIDE calls this], optional PATH, and whatever of the following runtime settings you want to include (COB_LIBRARY_PATH and COB_PRE_LOAD should be in; the list is what newer cobcrun --runtime-env shows):

```
Call environment
    COB_LIBRARY_PATH
    COB_PRE_LOAD
    COB_LOAD_CASE
    COB_PHYSICAL_CANCEL

File I/O
    COB_SYNC
    COB_LS_USES_CR
    COB_SORT_MEMORY
    COB_SORT_CHUNK
    COB_FILE_PATH
    COB_LS_NULLS
    COB_LS_FIXED
    COB_VARSEQ_FORMAT
    COB_UNIX_LF

Screen I/O
    COB_REDIRECT_DISPLAY
    COB_BELL
    COB_TIMEOUT_SCALE
    COB_SCREEN_EXCEPTIONS
    COB_SCREEN_ESC
    COB_LEGACY

Miscellaneous
    COB_SET_TRACE
    COB_TRACE_FILE
    COB_DISABLE_WARNINGS
    COB_ENV_MANGLE
```

As 2.0 (from next "official" source build on) will include an optional COB_RUNTIME_CONF [set via cobcrun --conf, too] that can include all these settings, it may is enough to have the "minimal" settings COB_LIBRARY_PATH and COB_PRE_LOAD in, together with the "additional command line options".

We may add "Check module loader" with "test module loader", too (the first using $module_loader --version, the second using $module_loader $module_loader_options TEST [given a previous compiled TEST.cob])

At now the latest published item in [releases](https://github.com/VUnit/vunit/releases) is 'v3.0.2': . However, https://github.com/VUnit/vunit/releases/latest is redirected to [v0.51.3](https://github.com/VUnit/vunit/releases/tag/v0.51.3). As a result, it is not straightforward to download the latest stable release programatically, i.e., `curl -s https://api.github.com/repos/vunit/vunit/releases/latest | grep "tag_name"`.

I believe this is not a critical issue, because most of the users will install the library through `pip`. Still, it might be worth checking the integration with travis-ci.

Unable to Create Index Aliases for ES 5.5 and Curator version 5.4 and Python 2.7

Error is **'`Failed to complete action: alias.  <type 'exceptions.UnicodeEncodeError'>: 'ascii' codec can't encode characters in position 26-27: ordinal not in range(128)
`**

Use case:

Current setup is a single daily index in Logstash, but now that index has grown too large, the user is looking to split out its document types into a few separate indices, eg. logstash_a.2015.11.13, logstash_b.2015.11.13 and logstash_c.2015.11.13 for example.

Because there are already a whole bunch of Kibana 4 dashboards built, they don't want to have to change the index pattern in K4 - because K4 doesn't currently make it easy for users to update all the existing visualizations to change the index pattern association.

Which means that they will have to change their LS config to fan out to A, B and C indices each day, and then create logstash-2015.11.13 (for example) as the name of the index alias that covers the 3 new indices, etc..  This will match what their Kibana index pattern expects.

The plan was for them to schedule the curator create alias command right after the new index rolls every day, so that the new alias (Eg. logstash-2015.11.13) is created.  This is because ES does not allow creating aliases against non-existing indices (so it has to be run after the index has rolled).  

The challenge here is that their admins will have to deal with daylight saving changes when scheduling the curator jobs.  Alternatives are to run curator on a separate client machine that is configured to use UTC time.  Or pre-create the next set of Logstash daily indices ahead of time.

Note that the 2nd approach above (pre-creating daily indices) will have to be done in a staggered way so that you don't end up with a ton of empty/future shards that will end up sharing the same indexing buffer with the current index (in 2.0, by default, it takes 5 mins for ES to mark a shard to be inactive - a direct sync flush will not prevent these shards from getting a portion of the indexing buffer).

There may be other use cases out there where having the ability to pre-create a bunch of empty/future Logstash indices will be helpful.

issue_title: Error when no indices were deleted
issue_body:
Hi all,

Is it normal that curator return an error when a valid command doesn't do anything?
For example:
```
/usr/local/bin/curator --loglevel WARN delete indices --older-than 6 --time-unit days --timestring %Y.%m.%d --prefix "foobar"
```
This command will return 0 if (at least) 1 indice is deleted. But it will return 99 if no indice is deleted.

I've just checked, it's not the behaviour of the old version.

It's a bit problematic when curator is used in a cron.
If, for a given reason, an indice is deleted manually, an email will be sent because logstash will fail.

issue_title: Support list of regexps for `exclude` configuration
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm running into a use case where we have a regex disabling the use of the ruby HTTParty library across (because we built a wrapper around it that handles our proxy settings). However, in some rare cases, we want to specifically use it (for instance because we're posting to localhost, or something in the internal network), and the exclude option for this hook has been growing and is not very elegant:
```yaml
- id: no_httparty
  exclude: ^(path_to_some_file_which_has_a_good_reason_to_use_httparty.rb|some_other_file.rb|...|...|...)$
```

It would be nice it there would be support for either:
- specifying `exclude` a list of patterns:
```yaml
- id: no_httparty
  exclude:
    - ^path_to_some_file_which_has_a_good_reason_to_use_httparty.rb$
    - ^some_other_file.rb$
    - ...
```
- supporting inline disable for pre-commit hooks (maybe in the way pep8 or rubocop do it)

Let me know if one of those options seems reasonable, I'd be happy to give a shot at a PR.

Thanks

issue_title: Reprojection of depth image giving incorrect values
issue_body:
I am trying to find the 3D point based on the depth scan. I need this to redirect kuka arm to a certain location (based on point identified on depth image)

I am following the standard projection formula where image pixel being reprojected is at (u,v) and focal length(f) is assumed to be the first element of Projection matrix at (0,0)

cx=320 // principle pixel x value
cy=240 // principle pixel y value
X=(z/f)*(u-cx)   // z is the depth value after converting to real world dimensions using [1]  
Y=(z/f)*(v-cy)
Z=z 
3D_PointinWorldFrame = inv(ViewMatrix)* [X;Y;Z;1] //

Questions
My 3D point is not matching where the object is placed and is off by significant amount. 
a) Is there any offset I am missing or are there any units incorrectly being used which may be causing this serious reprojection error?
b) Do you see any issues with the steps followed above Or are there any camera model bugs in the code?
(I chose the near value to be small 0.001  to avoid the issue #1197 )

`requests` does what I expect:

```py
>>> import requests
>>> requests.get('https://httpbin.org/get', params={'test': 'foo;bar'})
<Response [200]>
>>> _.json()['url']
'https://httpbin.org/get?test=foo%3Bbar'
```

So I figure the current behaviour must be a bug.

## Steps to reproduce

See above.

## Your environment

- OS: Ubuntu 16.04
- Python version: 3.6.1
- aiohttp version: 2.2.5
- yarl version: 0.12.0

We have about 20 new features according to `CHANGES` folder.
More than a month of development.
Let's make 3.1 release.
Release fast, release early!

Unfortunately the list of pending issues is still big (@thehesiod I remember about client timeouts, sure) but I see no reason to postpone the release.
We can implement pending tasks in backward compatible manner in aiohttp 3.2.

The release date is not scheduled yet, if you want to hop on the bus -- please hurry up (and write about your striving in a comment). I'll update blocker issues list.
